narrative: As a Black male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.30 and an LSAT score of 34, your profile also includes upper-middle family income and plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for most students in this dataset. The admissions model predicted a 56.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the scales just enough to reject your application since the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your academic record compared to typical students. The most influential factor pushing toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the lower tier relative to peers who tend to pass the bar. Next, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing because your 3.30 exceeds the typical 3.21 average, showing stronger overall college achievement than many. Your family's upper-middle income status had a smaller but negative pull, nudging the odds toward failure since students from similar brackets sometimes face unique pressures that correlate with bar struggles in the data. While your LSAT and full-time plans align closer to averages and didn't sway things much here, these top three elements—especially that low Year 3 decile—dominated the decision. Many admitted students have consistently higher decile rankings throughout undergrad, which helps them clear this hurdle. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting late-stage academic performance, perhaps through targeted prep courses or additional credentials that highlight improvement. Consider retaking the LSAT to push closer to or above the 36.7 average, as that could offset other concerns. Reaching out to admissions offices for feedback or exploring programs with holistic reviews might also open doors, especially those valuing diverse backgrounds like yours. With your solid GPA foundation, targeted steps could shift your predicted odds dramatically in the next cycle.
